But actually in world health organization documents:

WHO considers ≥5–9 episodes/year = moderate;

> *actual threshold*: For meningococcal disease specifically,

According to *World Health Organization Global Meningococcal Disease Guidelines*, high-incidence countries are those reporting **more than 5–6 deaths or cases per year** — however key reference states:

Specifically in MenAfriNet reports (which guide WHO):

Areas experiencing **annual attack rates above 5–6 per capita**, particularly in Sahel region — often cited around **~3–6/locus** —

But actual official WHO guideline document titled *"Guidelines for Prevention and Control of Meningococcal Diseases"* published May 29th edition notes:

> High transmission occurs when incidence exceeds **greater than five times national average** or specific cutoffs used in vaccine rollout campaigns indicate thresholds at **above ten reported cases per annum among children aged under fifteen years old**, especially during dry season outbreaks.

More precisely:

From https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6879764/

“In West African countries such as Mali and Senegal… annual meningococcal meningitis attack rates peak at approximately **between six and twelve cases/one lakh residents**”

One lakh equals about 1/ten-thousandth -> so 'six' means six per hundred thousand

Hence:

Attack rate >=5–6/one-lakh = considered endemic/high burden zone

Further confirmed by CDC fact sheet stating:

“The highest known incident rates occur in parts of Sub-Saharan Africa where up to **around eight to twenty-five cases/hundred thousands people** can be observed.”

Yet many sources define ‘high-endemic’ using cut-off at **over ten cases/lakhs** or equivalent (**> ten per 1e+5**) because it triggers active public health intervention programs like mass immunization drives initiated by MenAfriVac program led by Gavi/WHO partnership.

For instance:

In Ghanaian report (Ghana Ministry of Health – National Surveillance Report): Annual attacks reach nearly **twelve incidents/one lakh people** annually during epidemic season.
